exemplar.title:
To what extent does the framing effect affect an international population of 16 to
17-year-olds heading into leadership roles?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent does the framing effect affect an international population of 16 to
17-year-olds heading into leadership roles?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The aim is clearly stated and its relevance is thoroughly explained, linking framing biases to real-world leadership decisions.
The dual-processing model is accurately described and explicitly connected to how framing effects arise.
Both the independent and dependent variables are operationalized in the hypotheses in precise, testable terms.
weaknesses: Minor wording lapses in the opening paragraph could more tightly tie the definition of cognitive biases to the studyâs aim.
Background sample details (e.g. exact size and demographics) are somewhat vague.
The null hypothesis, while correctly stated, could more explicitly reference the operationalized DV measure.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All introduction strands meet the top-band descriptors: the aim is stated and its relevance is fully explained, the theory/model is described with clear linkage, and hypotheses operationalize variables. Only minor clarity issues prevent further critique, justifying full marks (6).

strengths: The independent-measures design is not only named but well explained with respect to demand characteristics and feasibility.
Relevant controls (age, residence, scenario equivalence) are identified and briefly justified.
Materials are clearly listed and tied to ecological validity through a realistic flood scenario.
weaknesses: The very small sample size (n=10) severely limits statistical power and external validity.
Self-selected sampling is described without sufficient discussion of associated bias.
Details on randomization procedures and researcher blinding are lacking.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Design, sampling, and materials all reach the âexplainedâ level in places, but significant methodological weaknesses (tiny N, sampling bias, procedural detail) constrain the overall quality to a mid-band score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Descriptive (bar chart) and inferential (Chi-square) statistics are appropriately chosen and correctly applied.
The graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis, with clear labels and accurate depiction of results.
Findings are interpreted with correct reporting of ÏÂ², degrees of freedom, N, p-value, and linked back to the hypothesis.
weaknesses: APA formatting of the chi-square result is not fully consistent with guidelines (e.g., expected frequencies not reported).
The bar chart could be enhanced with error bars or confidence intervals.
A few minor mislabels of which option is âcertainâ versus âriskyâ introduce momentary confusion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis meets the highest band descriptors for appropriate and accurate statistics, clear graphs, and hypothesis-linked interpretations. Small formatting and reporting details prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 is justified.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to the dual-processing model and contextualized against prior research.
Strengths and limitations are not only stated but explained in direct relation to validity concerns (e.g., small N, Hawthorne, cultural perceptions).
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to key limitations and carefully justified regarding how they improve power and validity.
weaknesses: The theoretical discussion, while present, lacks deeper critical analysis and could engage more with alternative models or citations.
A citation is needed to substantiate the claim about the Hawthorne effect (e.g., Perera, 2024).
The conclusion slightly overgeneralizes given the small and self-selected sample.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation shows strong performance in linking findings to theory, thoroughly explaining strengths/limitations, and justifying modifications. The moderate depth of theoretical critique and minor citation issues limit it to 5 rather than full marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart clearly depicts the two framings and both options, directly addressing the hypothesis.  Axes are labelled and the chart corresponds to the data table.  No substantive errors evident, earning a 5 within the top band.

The student reports ÏÂ², df, N, p-value, and compares to Î± and critical value, explicitly retaining the null hypothesis.  Interpretation is linked back to the hypothesis and framed in terms of chance.  This fulfills the highest descriptor; 5 awarded.

Both IV (positive vs. negative framing) and DV (option A vs. B chosen) are stated and operationalised; null and research hypotheses are written in testable form.  Operational wording is precise (percentage of participants choosing options).  This squarely fits the 5-6 band; a mid-band 5 is appropriate.

Self-selected sampling is defined and a justification (easy access to target age group in college) is given.  The choice of an international cohort is linked to generalisability, but the explanation of why self-selection was preferable to alternatives is brief.  This meets the lower end of âexplainedâ, so 3.

The dual-processing model is outlined (System 1 heuristics vs. System 2 logic) and explicitly connected to why framing should bias decisions.  The mechanism (loss aversion, cognitive miserliness) is tied to the present investigation, satisfying both description and linkage.  Some phrasing is textbook-like, but accuracy and connection are strong; this meets the 5-6 band, awarded 5.

Findings are related to the dual-processing model and previous research, noting inconsistency and potential Hawthorne effect.  Discussion moves beyond mere mention, yet depth of theoretical critique is moderate.  This aligns with âdescribed with reference,â high in 3-4 band; scored 4.

The student clearly states the aim-âto test the framing effect in an international sample of 16-17-year-oldsâand then justifies its relevance by linking it to future leadership decisions and possible education about biases.  The purpose is not merely identified but explicitly explained, fulfilling the descriptor for the top band.  Minor wording lapses do not detract from the clarity of the explanation, hence a solid 5 rather than 6.

Several strengths (international sample, independent design) and limitations (small N, Hawthorne, social desirability, cultural perceptions) are each explained in relation to validity and potential errors.  Relevance to the investigation is explicit, matching the 5-6 descriptor; a 5 is suitable.

Several controls (age, residence, identical wording, mathematical equality of options) are identified with short rationales.  Materials are listed and their relevance (flood scenario) is justified.  The explanation is present but not deeply analytical; hence a 3 within the top band.

Proposed modifications (larger sample, matched-pairs or stratified sampling, separate rooms, timed responses) are explicitly tied to corresponding limitations and justified regarding validity and type II error.  The criterion for full justification is met; hence 5.

The independent-measures design is not only named but explained with reference to demand characteristics and feasibility vis-Ã -vis other designs.  The rationale moves beyond description, satisfying âexplainedâ.  Minor depth limitations justify 4 rather than full marks.

Both descriptive (bar chart, percentages) and inferential (Chi-square) statistics are used appropriately for nominal, independent data.  Calculations appear accurate and the test selection is justified.  Minor formatting issues are negligible; thus 5.

annotations.comment: Controls of age, residence, and scenario equivalence are appropriate. Consider also controlling for prior exposure to similar scenarios or mood to reduce variability.
The opening paragraph defines cognitive biases, but it would strengthen the introduction to explicitly tie this definition back to why the framing effect is central to your studyâs aim.

The summary of Tversky & Kahnemanâs key study is useful background, but the description of âabout 300 undergraduatesâ is too vagueâspecify exact sample size and characteristics to improve clarity.
The investigation aim is clearly stated and its relevance to future leadership decisions is well explained, fulfilling the top band descriptor for aim and relevance.

The introduction provides a solid explanation of the dual-processing model and explicitly links System 1 heuristics and System 2 reasoning to the framing effect, demonstrating clear connection to the investigation.
The independent and dependent variables are correctly identified, but consider operationalizing the DV further by specifying the exact measure (e.g., percentage choosing each option) here.
The rationale for using an independent-measures design is thorough and explains how it reduces demand characteristics, demonstrating depth of understanding.
Consider discussing potential order effects or researcher blinding even in an independent-measures design to further bolster your methodological rigor.
The sample size of only 10 participants is extremely small; discuss how this limited N undermines statistical power and external validity.
The null hypothesis is stated correctly, but it lacks clear indication of the operationalized DV. Consider integrating the precise measure (option choice frequency) into this statement.
The research hypothesis is well written and operationalized, clearly stating expected differences in choice percentages across framing conditions.
Self-selected sampling is described but the risk of sampling bias isnât addressed. Acknowledge how self-selection might limit generalizability.
The procedure is generally clear, but more detail on how papers were randomized and distributed could further strengthen your control of researcher bias.
The bar chart is clear and directly addresses the hypothesis, but including error bars or confidence intervals would strengthen the visual presentation.
The material choice is well justified by recent flooding events, making the scenario salient for participants and boosting ecological validity.
The contingency table is accurate; however, reporting the expected frequencies would demonstrate that Chi-square assumptions are met.
APA reporting of the Chi-square result should follow the format ÏÂ²(df, N) = value, p = value. For example, ÏÂ²(1, N = 10) = .48, p = .49.
While percentage comparisons are described, the narrative occasionally mislabels which option is certain versus riskyâensure consistency in describing Option A and Option B.
The discussion links findings back to the dual-processing model, demonstrating a clear theoretical interpretation of non-significant results.
The Hawthorne effect is correctly identified as a limitation, but you should include an in-text citation for Perera (2024) to support this claim.
When discussing the risk of Type II error due to small groups, explicitly reference statistical power calculations to justify the need for a larger sample.
The note on cultural differences in loss perception is insightful; you could strengthen this by specifying which countriesâ contexts might influence responses.
The procedural and social desirability limitations are well articulated, demonstrating awareness of potential biases in your studyâs execution.
The conclusion overgeneralizes by suggesting all international teenagers are prepared for leadership; temper this claim given the small, self-selected sample.
